narrative:
First Warn Forecast
Friday, June 12, 2026


Sunshine and seasonable temperatures return today with much lower humidity.

Multiple rounds of thunderstorms are possible Saturday, including the risk for severe weather and flash flooding.

Cooler-than-normal temperatures arrive Sunday and continue into early next week.

After several days of heat, humidity, and thunderstorms, today brings a welcome change to the weather pattern. A cold front has moved through the region overnight, allowing cooler and drier air to settle across Missouri and Illinois. Skies will remain mostly sunny throughout the day, and temperatures will climb into the low to mid-80s, which is right where they should be for mid-June. Humidity levels will also be noticeably lower, making for a comfortable summer day whether you're heading outdoors, attending events, or simply enjoying a break from the recent muggy conditions.

Attention quickly turns to Saturday as an active weather pattern returns to the Mid-Mississippi Valley. Several disturbances moving through the upper-level flow will provide multiple opportunities for showers and thunderstorms throughout the day. Forecast confidence remains high that thunderstorms will develop, but the exact timing and intensity of each round remain somewhat uncertain.

One possible scenario involves a cluster of thunderstorms moving into the area during the morning hours. If this develops, some storms could produce damaging wind gusts. However, the bigger question is how these morning storms might impact the atmosphere later in the day. Should widespread morning storms occur, they could limit the amount of instability available for afternoon and evening development.

Regardless of what happens during the morning, confidence is increasing that another round of thunderstorms will develop along a cold front Saturday afternoon and evening. These storms will have access to warm, humid air and sufficient wind shear to organize into strong or severe thunderstorms. If instability is able to recover following any morning activity, widespread severe weather could develop across portions of the region.

The primary threat with Saturday evening's storms will be damaging straight-line winds. However, large hail, torrential rainfall, and a few tornadoes cannot be ruled out. Any isolated storms that develop ahead of the main line may be capable of producing all severe weather hazards before evolving into bowing segments where damaging winds become the dominant concern.

In addition to severe weather, flash flooding will be a concern Saturday afternoon and evening. The atmosphere will contain abundant moisture, and storms will be capable of producing very heavy rainfall in a short amount of time. Repeated thunderstorms moving over the same locations could lead to localized flooding, especially in areas that have already received significant rainfall during the past several days. Some locations could receive several inches of rain in only a few hours.

By Saturday night, the cold front will sweep through the area and bring an end to the severe weather threat from northwest to southeast. Cooler air will pour into the region behind the front, setting the stage for a refreshing end to the weekend.

Sunday and Monday will feel more like late spring than mid-summer. High temperatures will remain in the 70s, nearly 10 degrees below average for this time of year, with comfortable humidity levels and mostly dry conditions. Sunshine and pleasant weather will dominate much of the period.

Looking ahead, temperatures will gradually warm back toward normal by the middle of next week. A series of disturbances moving through northwesterly flow aloft may bring periodic chances for showers and thunderstorms beginning Tuesday and continuing through the latter half of the week.

Today is the calm before the storm. Enjoy the sunshine, comfortable temperatures, and lower humidity because a potentially active severe weather day is shaping up for Saturday. Stay alert for forecast updates as confidence increases on the timing and severity of Saturday's thunderstorms. Conditions improve significantly by Sunday with cooler temperatures and a much more comfortable air mass settling into the region.
